en.extract:
The South Georgia pipit (Anthus antarcticus) is a sparrow-sized bird found only on the South Georgia archipelago off the Antarctic Peninsula. It is the only songbird in Antarctica, South Georgia's only passerine, and one of the few non-seabirds found in the region. It builds nests from dried grass, usually within tussac grass, and lays four eggs a year. It feeds on small insects and spiders, and beach debris.


Taxonomy
The South Georgia pipit was formally described in 1884 by the German ornithologist Jean Cabanis based on a specimen collected on South Georgia. The specific epithet is derived from the Latin word antarcticus, meaning 'southern' or 'antarctic'. It is also known as the Antarctic pipit.


Description
The South Georgia pipit is a small and stocky pipit, 17 cm (6.7 in) long and weighing 36 g (1.3 oz). The species has long legs and a long hindclaw and a short tail. Its upperparts are dark brown to blackish, with cream- to buff-colored margins on the feathers making it look heavily streaked. The underparts are whitish or buff in color with dark streaks.


Conservation
It has been threatened by the human introduction of rats, mice, and reindeer to South Georgia, as well as environmental damage caused by humans. It has been chosen as the poster bird of the South Georgia Heritage Trust's Habitat Restoration (Rat Eradication) project, which started eradicating rats on South Georgia in 2011. The project's baiting phase ended in early 2015, and success was confirmed in 2018.
In 2000, the population was estimated to be 6000 to 8000 mature individuals. Prior to the restoration it was rare to see South Georgia pipits, most often on the rat-free Prion Island. Afterwards their numbers have increased noticeably and are much more often seen by visitors.


References


External links
BirdLife Species Factsheet

The '''South Georgia pipit''' ('''''Anthus antarcticus''''') is a sparrow-sized bird found only on the [[South Georgia Island|South Georgia]] archipelago off the [[Antarctic Peninsula]]. It is the only [[songbird]] in [[Antarctica]], South Georgia's only [[passerine]], and one of the few non-[[seabird]]s found in the region. It builds nests from dried grass, usually within [[Poa flabellata|tussac grass]], and lays four eggs a year. It feeds on small insects and spiders, and beach debris.<ref name=":0">{{Citation |last=Tyler |first=Stephanie |title=South Georgia Pipit (Anthus antarcticus) |date=2025-05-23 |work=Birds of the World |editor-last=Billerman |editor-first=Shawn M. |url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/sogpip1/1.1/introduction |access-date=2026-05-10 |publisher=Cornell Lab of Ornithology |language=en |doi=10.2173/bow.sogpip1.01.1 |last2=Boland |first2=Kelley C. |editor2-last=Keeney |editor2-first=Brooke K. |editor3-last=Rodewald |editor3-first=Paul G. |editor4-last=Schulenberg |editor4-first=Thomas S.|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

==Taxonomy==
The South Georgia pipit was [[Species description|formally described]] in 1884 by the German ornithologist [[Jean Cabanis]] based on a specimen collected on South Georgia.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Matschie |last2=Cabanis |date=1884 |title=Bericht über die April-Sitzung |url=http://link.springer.com/10.1007/BF02009930 |journal=Journal für Ornithologie |language=de |volume=32 |issue=2 |pages=252–254 |doi=10.1007/BF02009930 |issn=0021-8375}}</ref> The specific epithet is derived from the Latin word {{Lang|la|antarcticus}}, meaning 'southern' or 'antarctic'.<ref name=":0" /> It is also known as the Antarctic pipit.<ref name="HANZAB" />

==Description==
The South Georgia pipit is a small and stocky pipit, {{convert|17|cm|in|abbr=on}} long and weighing {{convert|36|g|abbr=on}}. The species has long legs and a long hindclaw and a short tail. Its upperparts are dark brown to blackish, with cream- to buff-colored margins on the feathers making it look heavily streaked. The underparts are whitish or buff in color with dark streaks.<ref name="HANZAB">{{cite book |editor1-last=Higgins |editor1-first=P. J. |editor2-last=Pater |editor2-first=L. M. |editor3-last=Cowling |editor3-first=J. S. |date=2006 |title=Handbook of Australian, New Zealand and Antarctic Birds |volume=7: Boatbill to Starlings |publisher=Oxford University Press |location=Melbourne |isbn=0-19-553996-6 |pages=1389–1391}}</ref><ref name=":0" />

==Conservation==
It has been threatened by the human introduction of rats, mice, and reindeer to South Georgia, as well as environmental damage caused by humans. It has been chosen as the poster bird of the South Georgia Heritage Trust's Habitat Restoration (Rat Eradication) project, which started eradicating rats on South Georgia in 2011. The project's baiting phase ended in early 2015, and success was confirmed in 2018.<ref name=":0" /><ref name="Acap2019" /><ref>{{cite news |last=Amos |first=Jonathan |date=2018-05-09 |title=Rodents driven from South Georgia |work=BBC News |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/science-environment-44046472 |access-date=2018-05-09 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180509000634/http://www.bbc.co.uk/news/science-environment-44046472 |archive-date=2018-05-09 |url-status=live }}</ref>

In 2000, the population was estimated to be 6000 to 8000 mature individuals.<ref>{{cite web |last1=BirdLife International |title=Species factsheet: ''Anthus antarcticus'' |url=http://datazone.birdlife.org/species/factsheet/south-georgia-pipit-anthus-antarcticus/details |website=Data Zone |access-date=10 March 2024 |date=2024}}</ref> Prior to the restoration it was rare to see South Georgia pipits, most often on the rat-free [[Prion Island]]. Afterwards their numbers have increased noticeably and are much more often seen by visitors.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Kenny |first1=Luke |title=A Detailed Guide to Recognizing the South Georgia Pipit |url=https://discover.silversea.com/destinations/antarctica/south-georgia-pipit/ |website=Discover by Silversea |access-date=10 March 2024 |date=17 February 2021}}</ref>

zh.extract:
南極鷚（学名：Anthus antarcticus），又稱南喬治亞鷚（英語：South Georgia pipit）是分布在英國海外領土南喬治亞與南桑威奇的南喬治亞島，南喬治亞島上唯一的雀形目，屬於鶺鴒科下的鷚屬，少數非海鳥分布在海鳥區域。
